title: How a Twitter Post Discovered a New Wasp Species to Japan
gist: A 2018 Twitter/X photo of a tiny wasp laying eggs on a praying mantis' egg case led a Kyushu University Museum entomologist to formally describe the male of Eupelmus curvator, a highly efficient parasitoid wasp new to Japan's scientific record, illustrating citizen science and taxonomic discovery via crowdsourced social-media observation.

insights.claim: A 2018 Twitter (now X) post by insect enthusiast Kanji Toyosaki, showing a tiny wasp laying eggs on a praying mantis' egg case, led Kyushu University Museum entomologist Taisuke Kawano to formally describe the male of a species new to Japan's record, Eupelmus curvator, for the first time.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A concrete case of social media functioning as an informal biodiversity-monitoring network, sourcing a formal taxonomic discovery at zero fieldwork cost — relevant to crowdsourced-science and citizen-data business models.
insights.quote: posted a photo to Twitter (now X) of a tiny wasp laying its eggs on a praying mantis’ egg case
insights.locator: In 2018, insect enthusiast and author Kanji Toyosaki

insights.claim: Eupelmus curvator was formally described in the journal Travaux du Muséum National d'Histoire Naturelle "Grigore Antipa," newly documenting the species in Japan despite it belonging to a well-known parasitoid wasp family.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
entities: Travaux du Muséum National d'Histoire Naturelle
insights.so_what: Shows how much undocumented biodiversity remains even in well-studied regions like Japan, surfaced through amateur observation rather than formal survey work.
insights.locator: who formally described the male of the species

insights.claim: Unlike most wasps in the family Eupelmidae, which lay their eggs in beetle larvae, Eupelmus curvator instead targets the egg cases (oothecae) of the narrow-winged mantis.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A notable behavioral divergence within the family, of interest to entomological and biocontrol research given the wasp's demonstrated efficacy against mantis egg cases.
insights.quote: Unlike most other eupelmids, however, Eupelmus curvator lays its eggs on other egg cases
insights.locator: The wasp belongs to the family eupelmidae

insights.claim: Researchers documented 77 Eupelmus curvator wasps parasitizing a single mantis ootheca, leaving only a handful of surviving mantis nymphs.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Quantifies a potentially strong natural population-control effect on mantis populations, a data point relevant to biocontrol and pest-ecology research.
insights.quote: the researchers discovered 77 wasps on a single ootheca with just a handful of surviving mantis nymphs
